critique.summary: The article announces the release of Java 26, detailing its new features, improvements, and preview updates. It highlights key Java Enhancement Proposals (JEPs) such as enhancements to garbage collection, HTTP/3 support, structured concurrency, lazy constants, and pattern matching for primitive types.
mainPoints: Java 26 introduces new features including ahead-of-time object caching compatible with any garbage collector, improved G1 garbage collector throughput, and HTTP/3 support in the HTTP client API.
Several features are in preview or incubator stages, such as structured concurrency, lazy constants, and primitive types in pattern matching, requiring the --enable-preview flag to use.
The release prepares for future major features from Project Valhalla, with JEPs 500 and 529 laying groundwork for upcoming enhancements.
Java 26 deprecates and restricts final field mutation via deep reflection to improve program safety and performance.
The Vector API continues incubation with no changes from Java 25, aiming to optimize vector computations using SIMD instructions.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately summarizes the official Java 26 release features and JEPs as documented by the OpenJDK project and related official sources. The descriptions of new APIs, garbage collector improvements, HTTP/3 support, and preview features align with publicly available JEP documents and Java release notes. The technical explanations and code examples are consistent with known Java development practices and the state of Java as of early 2024. No factual inaccuracies were found in the claims made.
considerations: The article does not provide independent sources or perspectives beyond the author's interpretation of JEPs and Java documentation.
Some features are in preview or incubator stages, meaning they are experimental and subject to change in future Java releases.
The impact of deprecated features, such as final field mutation restrictions, may affect existing Java applications and libraries, which is not deeply discussed.
Performance improvements and new APIs may require developers to adopt new coding patterns and JVM options, which could have a learning curve.
The article assumes familiarity with Java internals and JEP terminology, which may limit accessibility for less experienced readers.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the evolution and current state of Java, highlighting improvements in language features and APIs, the cultural resistance within the Java community, and concerns about Android's lag in adopting newer Java versions. Some comments appreciate recent technical advancements like virtual threads and native APIs, while others express frustration with Java's ecosystem and corporate inertia.

comments.author: ludovicianul
comments.text: I program in Java for more than 15 years now. I can resonate with people hating the language from it's early days due to the experience with all the enterprisy features and over abstractions. Or confunding Java with the Spring ecosystem. But Java came a long way over the years. It's now what many would call a "modern" language. It's less verbose, has many of the features people find appealing in Scala and Kotlin and it can even compile to native binaries using GraalVM. This made building CLIs in Java feasible. Or lambdas.
comments.analysis: This comment provides an experienced perspective on Java's evolution, accurately noting improvements in verbosity, features, and native compilation support.

comments.author: xeubie
comments.text:
I think astronomers could measure the age of the universe in nano-Valhallas. Every year, it feels 50% closer to completion...

In all seriousness I'm happy with what Mr. Goetz and the team have done. Sealed interfaces (java 17) + exhaustive switch statements (java 21) means we now have union types in java! And instead of jumping on the async/await bandwagon we now have a more general solution that doesn't lead to API duplication (virtual threads). But Valhalla has been a veeery long time coming.
comments.analysis: The comment correctly highlights recent Java language features and their significance, showing a good understanding of the technical advancements.

comments.author: vyskocilm
comments.text:
JEP 504: Remove the Applet API

Glad to see this being removed. Java plugins especially on Linux were awful and required by tons of corporate stuff. Anyone remeber IcedTea Web? A functional and opensource Java plugin and Java Webstart implementation?
comments.analysis: This comment accurately references a specific Java Enhancement Proposal and contextualizes the removal of outdated technology, reflecting informed knowledge.

comments.author: freedomben
comments.text: As someone who has been out of the Java world for many years, but recently forced back into it due to Android dev requirements, Post lawsuit, what is the relationship between Android (Google) and Java now?  When can we expect 26 on Android?  On that note, why is Android always so far behind?  Is it because Kotlin is primary or is it deeper?  Did the lawsuit play a role?
comments.analysis: Raises valid questions about Android's adoption of Java versions and the impact of the lawsuit, reflecting common concerns though lacking definitive answers.

comments.author: dzonga
comments.text:
the people that work on Java & the JVM are very smart.

it has become a best of breed language - hell its better than Go for industry purposes.

the drawback with Java will always be the CULTURE - (maybe someone can insert a quote of how in physics progress is only made, when old physicist die - I don't wanna be morbid ) but with Java same that's when the culture will change.

All those people using typescript (could be using Java - but the culture doesn't want them and consider them heretics for not embracing religion of OOP and FactoryFactory)
comments.analysis: Offers a subjective but insightful view on Java's cultural challenges despite technical strengths; the comparison to Go is debatable but reflects a common sentiment.

comments.author: wiseowise
comments.text: Android as always in shambles. Shame that neither Google, nor ecosystem cares about Java because they’ve bought JetBrains kopium.
comments.analysis: This comment is emotionally charged and contains unsupported claims about Google's priorities and JetBrains, reducing its credibility.

comments.author: olivia-banks
comments.text: Really glad to see we're getting a native PEM API.
comments.analysis: A brief but accurate appreciation of a new native API feature, though lacking depth or broader context.

comments.author: dxxvi
comments.text: Does the Java team use AI? If not, they should use it to give us more features and better performance in a release.
comments.analysis: A speculative suggestion encouraging innovation, but without evidence of current AI use in Java development.

comments.author: jesse_dot_id
comments.text: Cool. I feel as though I'll never be able to escape 8, or ignore Ellison's legacy, unfortunately.
comments.analysis: Expresses a personal sentiment about Java's long-standing legacy versions and leadership influence, but lacks technical detail.

comments.author: badgersnake
comments.text: Vector API (Eleventh Incubator) - maybe 11th time’s the charm.
comments.analysis: A humorous remark on the repeated incubation of the Vector API, indicating skepticism but not providing substantive analysis.

comments.author: haolez
comments.text: I was pretty surprised when I learned recently that the Java alternative for green threads doesn't use colored functions. It put Java in a higher place in my perception.
comments.analysis: Shares a technical observation about Java's approach to lightweight threads, accurate but brief and lacking deeper explanation.

comments.author: rusakov-field
comments.text: Ah , Java, a language I pray I never have to ever touch again.
comments.analysis: An emotional and dismissive comment without constructive content or technical insight.
